Database Internals: A Deep Dive into How Distributed Data Systems Work

Chapter 1: Storage Engines

At the heart of every database management system lies a storage engine — the component responsible for storing, retrieving, and managing data on disk and in memory. The design of a storage engine profoundly affects the performance characteristics of the database. Two primary families of storage engines dominate the landscape: B-Tree-based engines (optimized for read-heavy workloads) and LSM-Tree-based engines (optimized for write-heavy workloads).

B-Trees are the most widely used data structure for database indexes. A B-Tree is a self-balancing tree structure that maintains sorted data and allows searches, sequential access, insertions, and deletions in logarithmic time. Each node in a B-Tree can contain multiple keys and has multiple children, which makes B-Trees particularly well-suited for storage systems where data is read in blocks. The branching factor (the number of children per node) is typically chosen to match the page size of the storage device, minimizing the number of I/O operations needed to traverse the tree.

B+ Trees are a variant of B-Trees where all data is stored in leaf nodes, and internal nodes contain only keys for routing. This design has several advantages: leaf nodes can be linked together to support efficient range queries, internal nodes can have a higher branching factor (since they don't store data), and the tree height is typically shorter. Most modern database systems, including MySQL (InnoDB), PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQL Server, use B+ Trees for their primary indexes.

Log-Structured Merge Trees (LSM-Trees) take a fundamentally different approach. Instead of updating data in place (as B-Trees do), LSM-Trees buffer all writes in an in-memory sorted structure (typically a red-black tree or skip list) called a memtable. When the memtable reaches a certain size, it is flushed to disk as an immutable sorted run called an SSTable (Sorted String Table). Background compaction processes merge multiple SSTables to maintain read performance and reclaim space from obsolete entries. This approach converts random writes into sequential writes, which is significantly faster on both hard drives and SSDs.

The write amplification of a storage engine refers to the ratio of data actually written to disk compared to the data the application intended to write. B-Trees typically have lower read amplification but higher write amplification, while LSM-Trees have lower write amplification but potentially higher read amplification (because a read may need to check multiple SSTables). Space amplification measures the ratio of actual disk space used to the logical size of the data. These three metrics — read, write, and space amplification — form the fundamental trade-off space for storage engine design.

Chapter 2: B-Tree Variants and Optimization

Page management in B-Trees involves careful handling of splits and merges. When a node overflows (has too many keys), it must be split into two nodes, with the median key promoted to the parent. When a node underflows (has too few keys), it may need to be merged with a sibling or have keys redistributed from a sibling. These operations must be performed atomically to maintain the integrity of the tree, which is typically achieved through write-ahead logging (WAL).

Buffer management is critical for B-Tree performance. The buffer pool is a region of memory that caches frequently accessed pages from disk. When a page is requested, the buffer manager first checks whether it is already in the pool. If not (a cache miss), the page is read from disk and placed in the pool, potentially evicting another page. The choice of eviction policy (LRU, Clock, LRU-K, 2Q, ARC) significantly affects performance. Modern databases use sophisticated buffer management strategies that take into account access patterns, page types, and prefetching opportunities.

Copy-on-write B-Trees (used by systems like LMDB, BoltDB, and CouchDB) never modify pages in place. Instead, when a page needs to be updated, a new copy is created with the modification, and all ancestor pages up to the root are also copied to point to the new page. This approach eliminates the need for a write-ahead log (since the old tree remains intact until the new root is published) and naturally supports consistent snapshots (by keeping the old root accessible). However, it requires more disk space and can suffer from fragmentation.

Fractal Trees (used by TokuDB) combine aspects of B-Trees and LSM-Trees. Each internal node has a buffer that accumulates updates. When a buffer fills up, its contents are flushed to the appropriate child node. This amortizes the cost of updates across multiple operations, resulting in lower write amplification than traditional B-Trees while maintaining better read performance than LSM-Trees. Bw-Trees (used by Microsoft's Hekaton and Azure SQL) use a latch-free approach with a mapping table that maps logical page identifiers to physical locations, allowing lock-free concurrent access.

Chapter 3: Memory Management and Caching

Database buffer pools serve as the primary caching layer between the storage engine and the disk. The buffer pool is organized as an array of fixed-size pages (typically 4KB, 8KB, or 16KB), with a hash table for quick page lookup. Each page in the buffer pool has associated metadata including a pin count (the number of threads currently accessing the page), a dirty flag (indicating whether the page has been modified since it was read from disk), and usage statistics for the eviction algorithm.

Page eviction policies determine which page to remove from the buffer pool when space is needed for a new page. The Least Recently Used (LRU) policy evicts the page that has not been accessed for the longest time. However, pure LRU can be suboptimal for databases because a single sequential scan can flush the entire buffer pool. LRU-K addresses this by tracking the K most recent accesses and evicting the page whose Kth most recent access is the oldest. The Clock algorithm approximates LRU with lower overhead by arranging pages in a circular list and using a reference bit that is set when a page is accessed.

Write-ahead logging (WAL) ensures durability and atomicity of database operations. Before any modification is applied to the database pages, a log record describing the change is written to the log file on durable storage. This guarantees that the database can be recovered to a consistent state after a crash by replaying the log. The ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics) protocol is the standard recovery algorithm used by most database systems. It uses three passes during recovery: an analysis pass (to determine which transactions were active at the time of the crash), a redo pass (to bring the database to the state it was in at the time of the crash), and an undo pass (to roll back incomplete transactions).

Column-oriented storage is an alternative to the traditional row-oriented approach. Instead of storing all attributes of a record together (as in row storage), column storage stores all values of a single attribute together. This approach offers significant advantages for analytical queries that access only a subset of columns: it reduces I/O by reading only the needed columns, it enables more effective compression (because values of the same type are stored together), and it allows vectorized processing (where operations are applied to arrays of values rather than individual records). Systems like Apache Parquet, Apache Arrow, ClickHouse, and DuckDB use column-oriented storage.

Chapter 4: Transaction Processing

Transactions are the fundamental unit of work in a database system. A transaction groups multiple operations into a single logical unit that satisfies the ACID properties: Atomicity (all operations in the transaction either complete or none do), Consistency (the transaction moves the database from one valid state to another), Isolation (concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other), and Durability (once a transaction commits, its changes survive system failures).

Concurrency control mechanisms ensure that concurrent transactions produce results equivalent to some serial execution. Two-phase locking (2PL) is the most widely used approach: in the growing phase, a transaction acquires locks; in the shrinking phase, it releases them. Strict two-phase locking (S2PL) holds all locks until the transaction commits or aborts, which prevents cascading aborts. Multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) maintains multiple versions of data items, allowing reads to proceed without blocking writes. PostgreSQL, MySQL (InnoDB), Oracle, and SQL Server all use variants of MVCC.

Snapshot isolation is a popular concurrency control level that provides each transaction with a consistent snapshot of the database as of the start of the transaction. Read operations see the snapshot, and write operations are applied to the latest version. Write-write conflicts are detected and one of the conflicting transactions is aborted. Serializable snapshot isolation (SSI) extends snapshot isolation to detect and prevent write skew anomalies, providing full serializability. CockroachDB, YugabyteDB, and PostgreSQL (with the SERIALIZABLE isolation level) implement SSI.

Distributed transactions span multiple nodes and require coordination protocols. Two-phase commit (2PC) is the classic protocol: in the prepare phase, the coordinator asks all participants if they can commit; in the commit phase, if all participants voted yes, the coordinator tells them to commit. The main drawback of 2PC is that it blocks if the coordinator fails during the protocol. Three-phase commit (3PC) addresses this but is rarely used in practice due to its complexity and the availability of better alternatives. Saga patterns decompose long-running transactions into a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions for rollback.

Chapter 5: Distributed Database Architectures

Replication is the process of maintaining copies of data on multiple nodes. The primary purpose is to increase availability (the system continues to work even if some nodes fail) and to improve performance (by serving reads from local replicas). Single-leader replication designates one node as the leader that handles all writes, with followers receiving updates from the leader. Multi-leader replication allows writes on multiple nodes, which is useful for multi-datacenter deployments but introduces conflict resolution challenges. Leaderless replication (used by Amazon Dynamo, Cassandra, and Riak) allows any node to accept writes, using quorum reads and writes to ensure consistency.

Partitioning (also called sharding) divides a large dataset across multiple nodes, with each node responsible for a subset of the data. Range partitioning assigns contiguous ranges of keys to different partitions, which is efficient for range queries but can lead to hot spots. Hash partitioning distributes keys across partitions based on a hash function, which provides better load distribution but makes range queries expensive. Consistent hashing is a special form of hash partitioning that minimizes the amount of data that needs to be moved when nodes are added or removed.

Consensus protocols ensure that distributed nodes agree on values despite failures. Paxos, proposed by Leslie Lamport, was the first practical consensus algorithm but is notoriously difficult to understand and implement. Raft, designed as an understandable alternative to Paxos, separates the consensus problem into leader election, log replication, and safety. ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast) is used by Apache ZooKeeper for maintaining configuration information and distributed synchronization. Multi-Paxos and Multi-Raft extend single-decree consensus to agree on a sequence of values, which is essential for implementing replicated state machines.

Chapter 6: Consistency Models in Practice

Linearizability is the strongest single-object consistency model, requiring that operations appear to take effect instantaneously at some point between their invocation and response. It provides the illusion that there is only a single copy of the data, even though it may be replicated. Implementing linearizability requires coordination between nodes, which impacts performance and availability. The CAP theorem demonstrates that a distributed system cannot simultaneously provide consistency (linearizability), availability, and partition tolerance.

Causal consistency is a weaker model that only requires operations that are causally related to be seen by all nodes in the same order. Operations that are concurrent (not causally related) may be seen in different orders by different nodes. Causal consistency can be implemented without sacrificing availability during network partitions, making it an attractive choice for many applications. Vector clocks and their variants (version vectors, dotted version vectors) are commonly used to track causal dependencies.

Session guarantees provide consistency properties within the context of a single client session. Read-your-writes (also called read-after-write consistency) guarantees that after a write, the same client will always see the updated value. Monotonic reads guarantee that if a client has seen a value at time t, it will never see an older value in subsequent reads. These session guarantees can be implemented on top of eventually consistent systems, providing a practical middle ground between strong consistency and full eventual consistency.

Conflict resolution in distributed systems where concurrent updates are allowed requires strategies for determining the final value. Last-writer-wins (LWW) uses timestamps to resolve conflicts, discarding all but the most recent write. While simple, LWW can lose data. Application-specific conflict resolution pushes the resolution logic to the application layer, which can make more intelligent decisions. Conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs) are data structures designed to be merged automatically without conflicts, including counters, sets, registers, and sequences. CRDTs guarantee convergence without coordination, making them suitable for highly available systems.
